text: This is a video showing how I do my dissection for an inguinal hernia.
This is a right inguinal hernia.
The surgeon begins the dissection medially, approximately six to eight centimeters above the right inguinal hernia defect.
The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument to measure the area approximately six to eight centimeters above the right inguinal hernia defect.
The surgeon opens the hernia defect from end to end, measuring approximately two centimeters in length.
The surgeon continues the dissection for a right inguinal hernia, working laterally while maintaining a horizontal orientation.
The tendency is to cheat inferior as you go laterally, so try to avoid that.
The surgeon works medially within the retro-rectus space, carefully dissecting down towards the symphysis pubis.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the fenestrated bipolar near the bladder, ensuring precise handling to avoid damage in the retro-rectus space.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, maintaining a horizontal approach to expand the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument, angling it downward to grasp the edge of the peritoneum and pull it downward.
The surgeon continues dissection in the retro-rectus space, meeting the previously dissected lateral and medial components in the middle, ensuring alignment of the tissue planes.
The surgeon brings the medial and lateral dissections together, converging towards the hernia sac, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon works just above the hernia sac, using surgical instruments to manipulate the tissue in this area.
After completing the dissection, the surgeon moves inferiorly to continue the procedure, focusing on the area below the previously dissected hernia sac.
Inferior is likely gonna be the most difficult area for most because that's where the nerves and vessels are.
So be careful in that area, but it's very straightforward when you have such good visualization.
The surgeon reduces the indirect hernia defect, manipulating the hernia sac to reposition it within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon enters the fourth stage of dissection, focusing on the inferior portion near the hernia sac, where nerves and vessels are visible.
The surgeon positions the mesh approximately two centimeters inferior to the symphysis and extends it two centimeters across the midline, ensuring full coverage across the targeted area.
The bladder re-expands, visibly trapping the mesh in the surrounding area, complicating the dissection process in this region.

The surgeon clears the space directly over the femoral artery and vein, ensuring the area is prepared for the mesh to lie in the groove inferiorly, preventing the peritoneum from sliding under the mesh.
I try to follow Ed Felix's Ten Commandments for all of my inguinal hernias.
I'm sure you can find those on the internet, but it's to me very important that we all follow those steps on every single hernia.
The surgeon completes the dissection, preparing the surgical field for mesh placement to cover the defect effectively.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the vessels and cord structures near the femoral canal, using scissors to gently pull tissue away from the femoral vein.
The surgeon places the mesh flatly in the groove created during dissection, ensuring it covers the defect and prevents peritoneal sliding.
The surgeon measures at least six centimeters from the central portion of the defect to accommodate a twelve by sixteen mesh, preparing for its placement.
The myopectineal orifice is fully exposed, providing a clear view for the placement of the mesh.
